abstract: World Model, the supposed algorithmic surrogate of the real-world environment which biological agents experience with and act upon, has been an emerging topic in recent years because of the rising needs to develop virtual agents with artificial (general) intelligence. There has been much debate on what a world model really is, how to build it, how to use it, and how to evaluate it. In this essay, starting from the imagination in the famed Sci-Fi classic Dune, and drawing inspiration from the concept of "hypothetical thinking" in psychology literature, we offer critiques of several schools of thoughts on world modeling, and argue the primary goal of a world model to be simulating all actionable possibilities of the real world for purposeful reasoning and acting. Building on the critiques, we propose a new architecture for a general-purpose world model, based on hierarchical, multi-level, and mixed continuous/discrete representations, and a generative and self-supervision learning framework, with an outlook of a Physical, Agentic, and Nested (PAN) AGI system enabled by such a model.

organization_info.name: Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence

keyInsights: Information density, especially from abstract modalities like language, is more crucial for building world models than sheer sensory data volume, as it captures concepts unobservable through raw perception.

Mixed representations (discrete tokens for concepts, continuous embeddings for perception) are more robust and expressive than purely continuous representations for modeling complex world states, as formally supported by theoretical analysis.

Generative reconstruction objectives, grounded in observable data, are essential for preventing representation collapse and ensuring stability in learning world dynamics, unlike latent-reconstruction objectives which are prone to trivial solutions.
originalProblem: Existing "world models" often prioritize photorealistic video generation over functional utility for intelligent agents, leading to systems that lack generalizability for complex reasoning and planning.

Current world model approaches suffer from limitations in scope, abstraction, controllability, and interactability, failing to support the multi-modal, long-term reasoning required for real-world tasks.
Prevailing theoretical assumptions regarding data representation (purely continuous) and learning objectives (latent reconstruction) lead to brittle, ungrounded, or unstable model learning.
results: Theoretical analysis demonstrates that discrete language representations can be complete and efficient for complex data, with "scaling out" (sequence length) being more effective than "scaling up" (vocabulary size).
The paper formally proves that latent-reconstruction loss is prone to model collapse, while generative reconstruction loss grounded in a decoder prevents this, providing a stable learning signal.

The proposed PAN world model offers a blueprint for agentic reasoning capable of simulating and caching diverse future possibilities, enabling more deliberate, adaptive, and human-like planning for AI agents.
solution: The paper proposes the Physical, Agentic, and Nested (PAN) world model, a principled architecture designed for general-purpose simulation and reasoning capabilities.
PAN integrates multimodal data (e.g., text, video, touch) with mixed representations, using discrete tokens for symbolic reasoning and continuous embeddings for perceptual nuances.

It employs a hierarchical Generative Latent Prediction (GLP) architecture, combining an enhanced Large Language Model for abstract reasoning with diffusion models for perceptual dynamics, trained via a generative, observation-grounded objective.
paper_summary.summary: A paper critically evaluates existing world model (WM) paradigms, asserting their core function is to enable purposeful reasoning and action rather than realistic video generation. It introduces the Physical, Agentic, and Nested (PAN) world model, an architectural framework integrating multimodal data, mixed discrete-continuous representations, and hierarchical generative modeling to foster more robust and general-purpose artificial intelligence.

title: Critiques of World Models

abstract: World simulation has gained increasing popularity due to its ability to model virtual environments and predict the consequences of actions. However, the limited temporal context window often leads to failures in maintaining long-term consistency, particularly in preserving 3D spatial consistency. In this work, we present WorldMem, a framework that enhances scene generation with a memory bank consisting of memory units that store memory frames and states (e.g., poses and timestamps). By employing a memory attention mechanism that effectively extracts relevant information from these memory frames based on their states, our method is capable of accurately reconstructing previously observed scenes, even under significant viewpoint or temporal gaps. Furthermore, by incorporating timestamps into the states, our framework not only models a static world but also captures its dynamic evolution over time, enabling both perception and interaction within the simulated world. Extensive experiments in both virtual and real scenarios validate the effectiveness of our approach.

keyInsights: Combining token-level memory with explicit state cues (pose and timestamp) and state-aware attention enables flexible, high-fidelity long-term consistency in dynamic world simulations.
Relative state encoding simplifies the learning objective and improves stability for reasoning about spatiotemporal relationships across significant gaps.

Progressive memory sampling during training and confidence-based, similarity-filtered retrieval are critical for effectively managing and leveraging historical context in memory-augmented generative systems.
originalProblem: Existing video generative models suffer from limited temporal context windows, leading to spatial and temporal inconsistencies in long-term world simulations.
Current methods struggle with accurately reconstructing previously observed scenes after significant viewpoint changes or large temporal gaps, often 'forgetting' past states.
Geometric-based solutions are inflexible and prone to detail loss in dynamic, unbounded scenes, while implicit methods often lack visual fidelity or scalability.
results: WORLDMEM significantly outperformed baseline methods on the Minecraft benchmark, maintaining high quality (PSNR 23.98, LPIPS 0.1429, rFID 15.37) when generating hundreds of frames beyond the context window, where Diffusion Forcing severely degraded.

It demonstrated robust capabilities for modeling dynamic environmental changes (e.g., rain) and persisting user-placed objects or environmental features over hundreds of frames and extensive camera movements.
Achieved superior consistency across all metrics (PSNR 23.34, LPIPS 0.1672, rFID 43.14) on the RealEstate10K dataset, outperforming methods like Viewcrafter in real-world loop closure scenarios.
solution: WORLDMEM integrates a token-level memory bank, storing latent representations of memory frames along with their 5D poses and timestamps, into a conditional diffusion transformer.

It employs a state-aware memory attention mechanism where both query and key features are enriched with explicit, relative pose (Plücker embedding) and timestamp embeddings for robust spatiotemporal reasoning.

A confidence-based memory retrieval algorithm, utilizing FOV overlap and temporal proximity, is used to select the most relevant historical information, coupled with progressive sampling during training.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from S-Lab, Nanyang Technological University, along with collaborators from Peking University and Shanghai AI Laboratory, developed WORLDMEM, a framework for long-term consistent world simulation using an autoregressive conditional diffusion transformer. It integrates a token-level memory bank with state-aware attention to maintain spatiotemporal coherence, demonstrating superior performance in retaining object states and environmental dynamics across hundreds of frames on both Minecraft and RealEstate10K benchmarks.

title: WorldMem: Long-term Consistent World Simulation with Memory

abstract: World models predict state transitions in response to actions and are increasingly developed across diverse modalities. However, standard training objectives such as maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) often misalign with task-specific goals of world models, i.e., transition prediction metrics like accuracy or perceptual quality. In this paper, we present RLVR-World, a unified framework that leverages reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) to directly optimize world models for such metrics. Despite formulating world modeling as autoregressive prediction of tokenized sequences, RLVR-World evaluates metrics of decoded predictions as verifiable rewards. We demonstrate substantial performance gains on both language- and video-based world models across domains, including text games, web navigation, and robot manipulation. Our work indicates that, beyond recent advances in reasoning language models, RLVR offers a promising post-training paradigm for enhancing the utility of generative models more broadly. Code, datasets, models, and video samples are available at the project website: this https URL.

keyInsights: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) provides a highly efficient and effective post-training paradigm to align world models directly with task-specific metrics across diverse modalities.
Formulating world modeling tasks as a unified token-based autoregressive sequence generation problem enables the broad application of RLVR to both language and video.
Direct optimization with verifiable rewards can significantly reduce common generative model artifacts, such as repetition, and improve overall prediction quality with minimal training steps.
originalProblem: Traditional Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for world models often leads to a misalignment between training objectives and desired task-specific prediction goals.
MLE can result in artifacts like blurry video predictions or repetition in generated sequences and does not directly optimize for metrics like accuracy or perceptual quality.
A unified, efficient post-training method for diverse world models (language, video) to directly improve their utility for downstream tasks was lacking.
results: For language world models, RLVR-World improved text game state prediction accuracy by up to 44.8% and web page state prediction F1-score by 30.3% over supervised fine-tuning baselines, also boosting web agent success rates by 18.4%.
Video world models showed substantial gains on robot manipulation tasks, including a 9.2% relative LPIPS improvement and a 26.1% relative MSE improvement for multi-step prediction.

RLVR fine-tuning achieved these performance boosts with only a few hundred gradient steps, drastically more efficiently than the hundreds of thousands of steps for MLE pre-training, and reduced video repetition rates from 48.6% to 9.9%.
solution: A unified framework transforms language and video world modeling into an autoregressive token sequence generation task using decoder-only Transformers, initially pre-trained with MLE.

Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) is applied post-training, where the model generates multiple candidate predictions, and modality-specific decoders convert them back to the original form.

Rule-based, verifiable rewards are computed by directly comparing predictions against ground truth, and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is used to fine-tune the model without a separate value function.
paper_summary.summary: The Tsinghua University Machine Learning Group developed RLVR-World, a framework that fine-tunes pre-trained language and video world models using reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. This approach directly optimizes for task-specific prediction metrics, leading to improved accuracy by up to 44.8% in text game state prediction, a 30.3% F1 score increase for web page state prediction, and a 9.2% LPIPS improvement in robot video prediction, all while significantly reducing training steps and mitigating generative artifacts like repetition.

title: RLVR-World: Training World Models with Reinforcement Learning

abstract: World modeling has become a cornerstone in AI research, enabling agents to understand, represent, and predict the dynamic environments they inhabit. While prior work largely emphasizes generative methods for 2D image and video data, they overlook the rapidly growing body of work that leverages native 3D and 4D representations such as RGB-D imagery, occupancy grids, and LiDAR point clouds for large-scale scene modeling. At the same time, the absence of a standardized definition and taxonomy for ``world models'' has led to fragmented and sometimes inconsistent claims in the literature. This survey addresses these gaps by presenting the first comprehensive review explicitly dedicated to 3D and 4D world modeling and generation. We establish precise definitions, introduce a structured taxonomy spanning video-based (VideoGen), occupancy-based (OccGen), and LiDAR-based (LiDARGen) approaches, and systematically summarize datasets and evaluation metrics tailored to 3D/4D settings. We further discuss practical applications, identify open challenges, and highlight promising research directions, aiming to provide a coherent and foundational reference for advancing the field. A systematic summary of existing literature is available at this https URL

keyInsights: Native 3D and 4D representations offer crucial explicit geometry, metric information, and physical grounding, indispensable for advanced embodied AI and safety-critical applications.

Current generative models, while improving in fidelity across video, occupancy, and LiDAR modalities, still face challenges in maintaining physical plausibility, fine-grained control, and long-horizon consistency.
Standardized benchmarks and evaluation protocols are largely absent, hindering meaningful comparisons and robust progress in 3D/4D world modeling.
originalProblem: Prior generative modeling research largely emphasized 2D data, overlooking the native 3D/4D representations essential for real-world understanding in embodied AI.
The 'world model' literature suffered from fragmented efforts and inconsistent terminology due to a lack of precise definitions and a unified taxonomy.
A comprehensive survey dedicated to 3D and 4D world modeling, covering methodologies, datasets, and evaluation, was missing.
results: VideoGen models demonstrate advancements in visual quality (lower FID, FVD) but frequently struggle with temporal coherence and physical plausibility, limiting their direct utility for downstream perception and planning tasks.

Occupancy-based generation (OccGen) methods, particularly those using Triplane VAEs, achieve high reconstruction quality and improve 4D forecasting accuracy, leading to better planning performance and reduced collision rates.

LiDARGen models show increased fidelity to real LiDAR distributions, with progress in scene completion and rare condition modeling, though balancing point sparsity with structural consistency and addressing temporal dynamics are ongoing challenges.
solution: Introduced precise definitions for 'world models' and '3D/4D world modeling,' accompanied by a hierarchical taxonomy categorizing approaches by representation modality (VideoGen, OccGen, LiDARGen).
Presented a systematic review of generative and predictive paradigms, classifying models into functional types such as Data Engines, Action Interpreters, Neural Simulators, and Scene Reconstructors.

Systematically summarized relevant datasets and proposed a comprehensive set of evaluation metrics spanning generation quality, forecasting accuracy, and downstream performance for 3D/4D environments.
paper_summary.summary: This survey unifies the field of 3D and 4D world modeling by presenting the first comprehensive review, establishing a hierarchical taxonomy, precise definitions, and systematically summarizing diverse methodologies, datasets, and evaluation metrics. It outlines current applications in embodied AI and simulation while identifying key challenges and future research directions.

title: 3D and 4D World Modeling: A Survey

abstract: Embodied AI requires agents that perceive, act, and anticipate how actions reshape future world states. World models serve as internal simulators that capture environment dynamics, enabling forward and counterfactual rollouts to support perception, prediction, and decision making. This survey presents a unified framework for world models in embodied AI. Specifically, we formalize the problem setting and learning objectives, and propose a three-axis taxonomy encompassing: (1) Functionality, Decision-Coupled vs. General-Purpose; (2) Temporal Modeling, Sequential Simulation and Inference vs. Global Difference Prediction; (3) Spatial Representation, Global Latent Vector, Token Feature Sequence, Spatial Latent Grid, and Decomposed Rendering Representation. We systematize data resources and metrics across robotics, autonomous driving, and general video settings, covering pixel prediction quality, state-level understanding, and task performance. Furthermore, we offer a quantitative comparison of state-of-the-art models and distill key open challenges, including the scarcity of unified datasets and the need for evaluation metrics that assess physical consistency over pixel fidelity, the trade-off between model performance and the computational efficiency required for real-time control, and the core modeling difficulty of achieving long-horizon temporal consistency while mitigating error accumulation. Finally, we maintain a curated bibliography at this https URL.

organization_info.name: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: The proposed three-axis taxonomy effectively organizes hundreds of world model variants, providing a cohesive framework to understand their design choices and trade-offs.

An evolutionary trend from compact global latent vector representations (e.g., RSSMs) towards token feature sequences (e.g., Transformers with LLMs) and explicit 3D rendering representations (e.g., NeRF, 3D Gaussian Splatting) is observed.
There is a growing convergence between large-scale generative models and embodied AI, enabling general-purpose simulation and pre-training for diverse downstream applications.
originalProblem: The rapid proliferation of diverse world model variants in Embodied AI leads to inconsistent terminology and a fragmented understanding across different research communities.
A lack of a unified taxonomy hinders systematic comparison, classification, and clear communication within the field.
The heterogeneous nature of data resources and evaluation metrics across sub-domains (robotics, autonomous driving, video generation) impedes standardized assessment and reproducibility.
results: Quantitative comparisons demonstrated improved visual fidelity (FID) and temporal consistency (FVD) in recent diffusion-based models for video generation, particularly in autonomous driving benchmarks like nuScenes.

Models utilizing explicit 3D geometric priors (e.g., BEV, voxel grids, 3D Gaussian Splatting) achieved superior performance in 4D occupancy forecasting (mIoU) and robotic manipulation tasks (Success Rate).

World models increasingly enable sample-efficient learning in control tasks (e.g., DMC), while fundamental challenges such as developing unified datasets, physically consistent metrics, and achieving long-horizon temporal consistency persist.
solution: Proposed a novel three-axis taxonomy to categorize world models based on Functionality (Decision-Coupled vs. General-Purpose), Temporal Modeling (Sequential Simulation vs. Global Difference), and Spatial Representation (Latent Vector, Token Sequence, Latent Grid, Decomposed Rendering).
Formalized the problem setting of world models within Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) and detailed their learning objectives via the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO).

Systematized data resources and evaluation metrics into comprehensive categories, including simulation platforms, interactive benchmarks, offline datasets, real-world robot platforms, and multi-level performance metrics.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Nankai University and a collaborative network introduce a unified three-axis taxonomy for world models in Embodied AI, classifying them by functionality, temporal modeling, and spatial representation. This comprehensive survey systematically organizes diverse approaches, data resources, and evaluation metrics, providing a structured understanding and identifying critical future research directions.

title: A Comprehensive Survey on World Models for Embodied AI

abstract: This paper describes our research on AI agents embodied in visual, virtual or physical forms, enabling them to interact with both users and their environments. These agents, which include virtual avatars, wearable devices, and robots, are designed to perceive, learn and act within their surroundings, which makes them more similar to how humans learn and interact with the environments as compared to disembodied agents. We propose that the development of world models is central to reasoning and planning of embodied AI agents, allowing these agents to understand and predict their environment, to understand user intentions and social contexts, thereby enhancing their ability to perform complex tasks autonomously. World modeling encompasses the integration of multimodal perception, planning through reasoning for action and control, and memory to create a comprehensive understanding of the physical world. Beyond the physical world, we also propose to learn the mental world model of users to enable better human-agent collaboration.

keyInsights: True intelligence in AI requires grounding in real-world interaction, moving beyond disembodied models.
World models are essential for robust reasoning, planning, and overcoming the limitations (inefficiency, hallucination) of purely generative models in embodied contexts.
Proactive consideration of ethical implications like privacy and anthropomorphism is critical for responsible embodied AI development.
originalProblem: Existing 'disembodied' AI systems, like large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs), lack a fundamental understanding of the physical world and direct means of interaction.

Generative models are often inefficient and prone to hallucination when applied to embodied tasks, as their next-token/pixel prediction objective often misses essential information for robust reasoning and planning.
There is a need to develop AI agents capable of natural, intuitive interaction with both users and environments, similar to human learning and engagement.
results: Meta AI's new benchmarks reveal significant performance gaps: SOTA video-language models achieve 40.2% on Minimal Video Pairs (MVP) compared to 92.9% human, and 38% on WorldPrediction-PP (procedural planning) versus near-perfect human scores.
Perception Language Models (PLMs) demonstrate state-of-the-art visual understanding, with improvements over open models (e.g., +39.8 CIDEr for video captioning).
Vision-Language World Models (VLWM) for wearable agents show improved planning assistance (e.g., +20% success rate, +10% mean action accuracy) over prompting-based baselines.
solution: Meta AI proposes developing embodied AI agents centered on a 'world modeling' approach, which enables agents to understand, simulate, and reason about their environment.

The approach integrates multimodal perception (image, video, audio, speech, touch) and focuses on building distinct 'physical world models' (e.g., JEPA-based in latent space) and 'mental world models' (for human intentions).

The research involves creating novel, rigorous benchmarks (e.g., Minimal Video Pairs, IntPhys 2, CausalVQA, WorldPrediction) to evaluate physical and causal reasoning capabilities beyond traditional metrics.
paper_summary.summary: Meta AI Research proposes a world-modeling approach for embodied AI agents, arguing it addresses limitations of current generative models by enabling agents to understand, simulate, and reason about physical and mental environments. Their work highlights existing AI models' struggles on new benchmarks for physical and causal reasoning while demonstrating improved performance in visual understanding and planning assistance with their integrated systems.

title: Embodied AI Agents: Modeling the World

abstract: The concept of world models has garnered significant attention due to advancements in multimodal large language models such as GPT-4 and video generation models such as Sora, which are central to the pursuit of artificial general intelligence. This survey offers a comprehensive review of the literature on world models. Generally, world models are regarded as tools for either understanding the present state of the world or predicting its future dynamics. This review presents a systematic categorization of world models, emphasizing two primary functions: (1) constructing internal representations to understand the mechanisms of the world, and (2) predicting future states to simulate and guide decision-making. Initially, we examine the current progress in these two categories. We then explore the application of world models in key domains, including generative games, autonomous driving, robotics, and social simulacra, with a focus on how each domain utilizes these aspects. Finally, we outline key challenges and provide insights into potential future research directions. We summarize the representative papers along with their code repositories in this https URL.

keyInsights: World models primarily serve two functions: constructing implicit internal representations of world mechanisms for informed decision-making, and explicitly simulating and forecasting future states of the world, often through visual data.

Large Language Models (LLMs) and Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) are emerging as powerful backbones for acquiring and leveraging extensive latent 'world knowledge' encompassing common sense, spatial, temporal, and social understanding.

The field is rapidly moving towards creating fully interactive and embodied 3D simulations, shifting from passive video generation to physically constrained, first-person, action-conditioned future world predictions.
originalProblem: Existing literature lacked a systematic definition of world models and a comprehensive analysis of their diverse requirements across applications.
A persistent debate exists regarding whether a world model's core function is to understand the current world state or primarily to predict future states for decision-making.
Recent capabilities of large multimodal AI models necessitate a re-evaluation of what constitutes a 'world model' and how current AI systems align with this concept.
results: Provided a comprehensive overview of world model applications, demonstrating their potential in game intelligence, embodied intelligence (robotics), urban intelligence (autonomous driving), and societal intelligence (social simulacra).

Identified critical open problems for world models, including ensuring robust physical rule adherence, enriching the social dimension, developing standardized benchmarks, and bridging the simulation-to-reality gap.
Highlighted the need to address ethical and safety concerns related to data privacy, the potential for simulating unsafe scenarios, and accountability for AI-generated content.
solution: Introduced a novel categorization of world models based on two primary functions: constructing implicit representations to understand the external world and predicting future states to simulate and guide decision-making.
Contextualized modern world model advancements within a historical narrative, tracing their evolution from early cognitive theories to contemporary deep learning paradigms.

Systematically examined how these dual functionalities of world models are leveraged across diverse application domains, including generative games, embodied intelligence, urban intelligence, and societal intelligence.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tsinghua University present a comprehensive survey of world models, proposing a dual categorization based on understanding implicit world representations and predicting future states. The work analyzes world model applications across generative games, robotics, autonomous driving, and social simulacra, while outlining critical open challenges.

title: Understanding World or Predicting Future? A Comprehensive Survey of World Models

abstract: Agentic reinforcement learning (ARL) has rapidly gained attention as a promising paradigm for training agents to solve complex, multi-step interactive tasks. Despite encouraging early results, ARL remains highly unstable, often leading to training collapse. This instability limits scalability to larger environments and longer interaction horizons, and constrains systematic exploration of algorithmic design choices. In this paper, we first propose ARLArena, a stable training recipe and systematic analysis framework that examines training stability in a controlled and reproducible setting. ARLArena first constructs a clean and standardized testbed. Then, we decompose policy gradient into four core design dimensions and assess the performance and stability of each dimension. Through this fine-grained analysis, we distill a unified perspective on ARL and propose SAMPO, a stable agentic policy optimization method designed to mitigate the dominant sources of instability in ARL. Empirically, SAMPO achieves consistently stable training and strong performance across diverse agentic tasks. Overall, this study provides a unifying policy gradient perspective for ARL and offers practical guidance for building stable and reproducible LLM-based agent training pipelines.

keyInsights: Agentic Reinforcement Learning (ARL) training is highly sensitive to Importance Sampling (IS) design, where tolerant clipping methods often lead to training collapse driven by negative-advantage sequences with low IS ratios.
Incorporating fine-grained environmental advantage (e.g., GIGPO) generally enhances performance by providing richer information and mitigating reward sparsity in multi-turn environments.
Policy gradient optimization for agentic tasks is sensitive to the degree of off-policy staleness, with lower staleness consistently yielding higher performance.
originalProblem: Autonomous agents using Large Language Models (LLMs) for complex, multi-step interactive tasks frequently suffer from inherent training instability.
This instability leads to training collapse, hindering scalability to larger environments and longer interaction horizons.
Challenges include the generation of invalid actions, sparse rewards, difficulties in long-horizon credit assignment, and non-stationary agent-environment dynamics.
results: Sequence masking, applied to harmful negative-advantage sequences with low IS ratios, effectively stabilized training for previously unstable methods, improving their success rates significantly (e.g., CISPO from 54.42% to 78.88% on ALFWorld).
The proposed SAMPO algorithm consistently achieved stable training and an average 25.2% performance improvement over the GRPO baseline across diverse agentic tasks.

A SAMPO-trained open-source model (Qwen3-4B-RFT) demonstrated higher success rates (92.72% on ALFWorld) compared to larger closed-source models (GPT-5.2 at 51.56%) and multi-agent systems, highlighting the impact of stable RL training.
solution: Proposes ARLArena, a unified framework designed for systematic analysis and stable training, built upon a standardized testbed with incremental stabilization strategies like behavior cloning and format penalties.

Decomposes policy gradient methods into four core design dimensions: Loss Aggregation, Importance Sampling (IS) clipping, Trajectory Filtering and Resampling, and Advantage Design, enabling fine-grained analysis.

Develops SAMPO (Stable Agentic Multi-turn Policy Optimization), an algorithm that integrates identified stable design choices, such as sequence-level clipping and fine-grained advantage estimation, to mitigate instability.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from UCLA introduce ARLArena, a unified framework for systematically analyzing and stabilizing Agentic Reinforcement Learning (ARL) for Large Language Model agents. The framework proposes the Stable Agentic Multi-turn Policy Optimization (SAMPO) algorithm, which improves ARL training stability and achieves a 25.2% average performance improvement over baselines across diverse interactive tasks, even outperforming larger closed-source models.

title: ARLArena: A Unified Framework for Stable Agentic Reinforcement Learning

abstract:
Embodied multimodal large models (EMLMs) have gained significant attention in
recent years due to their potential to bridge the gap between perception,
cognition, and action in complex, real-world environments. This comprehensive
review explores the development of such models, including Large Language Models
(LLMs), Large Vision Models (LVMs), and other models, while also examining
other emerging architectures. We discuss the evolution of EMLMs, with a focus
on embodied perception, navigation, interaction, and simulation. Furthermore,
the review provides a detailed analysis of the datasets used for training and
evaluating these models, highlighting the importance of diverse, high-quality
data for effective learning. The paper also identifies key challenges faced by
EMLMs, including issues of scalability, generalization, and real-time
decision-making. Finally, we outline future directions, emphasizing the
integration of multimodal sensing, reasoning, and action to advance the
development of increasingly autonomous systems. By providing an in-depth
analysis of state-of-the-art methods and identifying critical gaps, this paper
aims to inspire future advancements in EMLMs and their applications across
diverse domains.

organization_info.name: Guangdong Laboratory of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Economy

keyInsights: EMLMs are increasingly integrating LLMs and LVLMs to enhance capabilities in embodied perception, navigation, and interaction.
Simulation environments and diverse, high-quality multimodal datasets are crucial for training and evaluating EMLMs.
Key challenges include cross-modal alignment, computational efficiency, generalization, temporal information handling, and dataset diversity.
originalProblem: Existing literature reviews often focus narrowly on LLMs or LVMs, overlooking their integration with embodied agents.
Previous reviews either covered embodied intelligence too broadly or too narrowly, lacking a 'full-stack' analysis.
A systematic and timely overview of Embodied Multimodal Large Models (EMLMs) was needed to consolidate rapid advancements.
results: The review systematically categorizes EMLM development based on embodied perception, navigation, and interaction, detailing various models and approaches.
It provides a comprehensive overview of foundational models (LLMs, LVMs, LVLMs) and their role in EMLMs.
It summarizes critical benchmark datasets and simulation environments essential for EMLM research and development.
solution: The paper conducts the first systematic review of Embodied Multimodal Large Models (EMLMs).
It analyzes approximately 300 research papers to provide a 'full-stack' understanding of EMLMs, covering foundational models, embodied components, simulation, and datasets.
It identifies current main findings, key challenges, and promising future research directions for the field.
paper_summary.summary: This paper presents a comprehensive review of Embodied Multimodal Large Models (EMLMs), analyzing their development, integration of large language and vision models, and the role of datasets and simulation environments. It highlights advancements in embodied perception, navigation, and interaction, while outlining persistent challenges and future research directions.

title: Exploring Embodied Multimodal Large Models: Development, Datasets, and  Future Directions

abstract:
World Models help Artificial Intelligence (AI) predict outcomes, reason about
its environment, and guide decision-making. While widely used in reinforcement
learning, they lack the structured, adaptive representations that even young
children intuitively develop. Advancing beyond pattern recognition requires
dynamic, interpretable frameworks inspired by Piaget's cognitive development
theory. We highlight six key research areas -- physics-informed learning,
neurosymbolic learning, continual learning, causal inference, human-in-the-loop
AI, and responsible AI -- as essential for enabling true reasoning in AI. By
integrating statistical learning with advances in these areas, AI can evolve
from pattern recognition to genuine understanding, adaptation and reasoning
capabilities.

organization_info.name: University of Natural Resources and Life Sciences, Vienna

keyInsights: Effective world models require combining statistical learning with structured reasoning
Physical laws and embodied experiences are crucial for grounded understanding
Human-in-the-loop approaches are essential for guiding learning and ensuring alignment
originalProblem: Current AI systems lack true reasoning abilities and struggle with causal understanding
Large language models are limited to pattern recognition without deeper comprehension of the world
results: Identifies key research directions including causal inference and open-world learning
Provides theoretical foundation for developing more robust and interpretable AI systems
Establishes framework for advancing beyond pure statistical approaches to achieve true reasoning capabilities
solution: Framework based on Piaget's cognitive development theory for active knowledge construction
Integration of physics-informed machine learning, neurosymbolic AI, and human expertise
paper_summary.summary: Researchers propose a framework for building AI systems inspired by Piaget's theory of cognitive development, emphasizing the integration of physics-informed learning, causal reasoning, and human-in-the-loop approaches to move beyond the pattern recognition limitations of current large language models toward more robust and interpretable world modeling capabilities.

title: World Models in Artificial Intelligence: Sensing, Learning, and  Reasoning Like a Child

abstract: This is not a typical survey of world models; it is a guide for those who want to build worlds. We do not aim to catalog every paper that has ever mentioned a ``world model". Instead, we follow one clear road: from early masked models that unified representation learning across modalities, to unified architectures that share a single paradigm, then to interactive generative models that close the action-perception loop, and finally to memory-augmented systems that sustain consistent worlds over time. We bypass loosely related branches to focus on the core: the generative heart, the interactive loop, and the memory system. We show that this is the most promising path towards true world models.

keyInsights: True world models require the deliberate integration of a generative heart, an interactive loop, and a persistent memory system to achieve dynamic, interactive realities.
The field's progression can be understood through a five-stage evolutionary roadmap, where advancements in one stage lay the foundation for the next.
Achieving true world models hinges on addressing three fundamental challenges: coherence (evaluation), compression (scaling), and alignment (safety).
originalProblem: A lack of clear, unifying consensus on what constitutes a "true world model" across diverse AI subfields.
Fragmented research efforts often optimize narrow tasks without a coherent vision for interactive and persistent simulated worlds.
Inconsistent definitions and applications of the term "world model" have led to ambiguity.
results: Articulated a precise architectural definition of a "true world model" comprising three indispensable subsystems: Generative Heart, Interactive Loop, and Memory System.
Established a five-stage evolutionary roadmap guiding the development of world models, categorizing existing and future advancements within each stage.

Identified emergent properties for true world models
									—Persistence, Agency, and Emergence—and defined three frontier challenges that must be overcome (Coherence, Compression, Alignment).
solution: Proposes an opinionated "Hitchhiker's Guide" that synthesizes disparate research into a coherent, evolutionary roadmap for world models.
Establishes a precise, architectural definition of a true world model, integrating a generative heart, an interactive loop, and a persistent memory system.
Outlines a five-stage progression, from mask-based models to unified architectures, interactive generative models, memory-augmented systems, and finally, "True World Models."
paper_summary.summary: A conceptual framework and evolutionary roadmap are proposed for building "true world models," integrating a generative core, an interactive loop, and a persistent memory system into a five-stage progression. The framework aims to unify fragmented research efforts and define key architectural commitments necessary for creating dynamic, interactive, and self-sustaining computational worlds.

title: From Masks to Worlds: A Hitchhiker's Guide to World Models

abstract: While world models have emerged as a cornerstone of embodied intelligence by enabling agents to reason about environmental dynamics through action-conditioned prediction, their evaluation remains fragmented. Current evaluation of embodied world models has largely focused on perceptual fidelity (e.g., video generation quality), overlooking the functional utility of these models in downstream decision-making tasks. In this work, we introduce WorldArena, a unified benchmark designed to systematically evaluate embodied world models across both perceptual and functional dimensions. WorldArena assesses models through three dimensions: video perception quality, measured with 16 metrics across six sub-dimensions; embodied task functionality, which evaluates world models as data engines, policy evaluators, and action planners integrating with subjective human evaluation. Furthermore, we propose EWMScore, a holistic metric integrating multi-dimensional performance into a single interpretable index. Through extensive experiments on 14 representative models, we reveal a significant perception-functionality gap, showing that high visual quality does not necessarily translate into strong embodied task capability. WorldArena benchmark with the public leaderboard is released at this https URL, providing a framework for tracking progress toward truly functional world models in embodied AI.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: A significant "perception–functionality gap" exists, indicating that high visual quality in world models does not consistently translate to strong performance in embodied tasks.
General video models excel in perceptual realism but often lack physical adherence, while embodied models show better consistency in structure and interaction-related metrics.
Current world models struggle to serve as robust data engines, policy evaluators, or action planners, generally lagging behind real data or specialized policies in functional tasks.
originalProblem: Current evaluation protocols for embodied world models are fragmented, focusing predominantly on perceptual fidelity without a holistic view of their practical utility.
Existing benchmarks fail to assess the critical functional roles of world models in downstream embodied tasks like synthetic data generation, policy evaluation, or action planning.
Model coverage is insufficient, with many evaluations focusing on general video generation models while overlooking specialized robot- and action-conditioned world models.
results: General video models like Veo 3.1 achieved the highest scores in visual quality and aesthetics, while action-conditioned embodied models (e.g., CtrlWorld) showed better physical adherence and higher human-rated win rates.

The EWMScore correlated strongly with human judgments on perceptual quality (Pearson r = 0.825) but only moderately with data synthesis performance (r = 0.600) and weakly with action planning performance (r = 0.360).

CtrlWorld exhibited a strong correlation (Pearson r = 0.986) as an embodied policy evaluator, but overall, synthetic data from world models provided limited gains for policy training compared to real data, and action planning capabilities were substantially lower than specialized VLA policies.
solution: WorldArena introduces a unified benchmark using the RobotTwin 2.0 dataset to comprehensively evaluate 14 representative embodied world models.
A multi-faceted evaluation framework combines 16 objective metrics across 6 perceptual quality dimensions with assessments of functional utility in three embodied tasks.
The framework incorporates subjective human evaluation and proposes EWMScore, an interpretable, unified metric for generative performance.
paper_summary.summary: A new benchmark, WorldArena, unifies the evaluation of embodied world models by assessing both their perceptual fidelity and functional utility in robotic manipulation tasks. The benchmark, evaluating 14 models, identifies a "perception–functionality gap" where models with high visual quality often do not perform well in practical embodied applications.

title: WorldArena: A Unified Benchmark for Evaluating Perception and Functional Utility of Embodied World Models

abstract: The Driving World Model (DWM), which focuses on predicting scene evolution during the driving process, has emerged as a promising paradigm in the pursuit of autonomous driving (AD). DWMs enable AD systems to better perceive, understand, and interact with dynamic driving environments. In this survey, we provide a comprehensive overview of the latest progress in DWM. First, we review the DWM ecosystem, which is constructed using mainstream simulators, high-impact datasets, and various metrics that evaluate DWMs across multiple dimensions. We then categorize existing approaches based on the modalities of the predicted scenes, including video, point cloud, occupancy, latent feature, and traffic map, and summarize their specific applications in AD research. In addition, the performance of representative approaches across generating and driving tasks is presented. Finally, we discuss the potential limitations of current research and propose future directions. This survey provides valuable insights into the development and application of DWM, fostering its broader adoption in AD. The relevant papers are collected at this https URL.

author_info.institution: Huazhong University of Science and Technology
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Driving World Models enable proactive, foresight-driven cognition by predicting future scene evolution, moving beyond reactive control, and addressing data bottlenecks through self-supervised learning.
DWMs are categorized by their prediction modalities, each presenting unique advantages, inherent challenges, and specific development pathways in fidelity, efficiency, and real-world applicability.

The integration of DWMs is transforming autonomous driving by enabling flexible neural simulators, cost-effective data generation, enhanced planning capabilities, and robust pre-training paradigms for comprehensive world representations.
originalProblem: Autonomous Driving (AD) systems face challenges from complex environmental dynamics, stringent safety requirements, and a scarcity of high-quality annotated data, leading to limited scalability and understanding.
Traditional perception systems struggle with category-agnostic understanding of dynamic environments and rely heavily on manual annotations.

The rapidly growing and fragmented research landscape for Driving World Models (DWMs) lacked a unified, comprehensive classification that addresses diverse technical challenges and specific implementations.
results: Advanced diffusion-based DWMs achieve photorealistic, controllable, and spatiotemporally consistent long-term video generation across diverse environments.

4D DWMs demonstrate progress in generating physically realistic and temporally consistent point clouds and volumetric occupancy, with models unifying diverse tasks like scene prediction and behavior planning.

DWMs show superior performance in open-loop planning tasks (e.g., improved L2 distance, reduced collision rates) compared to traditional end-to-end frameworks, and outperform some traditional methods in closed-loop driving benchmarks.
solution: The paper provides a structured, comprehensive survey of Driving World Models (DWMs) for autonomous driving.
It systematically categorizes DWM approaches based on their prediction modalities, including visual, 4D (point cloud, occupancy), multimodal, latent, and vectorized spaces.
The survey analyzes the DWM ecosystem (simulators, datasets, metrics), applications (simulation, data generation, enhancing driving, pre-training), and evaluates performance across tasks.
paper_summary.summary: A comprehensive survey from Huazhong University of Science and Technology and Baidu Inc. reviews Driving World Models (DWMs), classifying their diverse prediction modalities and applications in autonomous driving. It identifies technical challenges and outlines future research directions for leveraging DWMs to enhance environmental understanding, data efficiency, and safety.

title: The Role of World Models in Shaping Autonomous Driving: A Comprehensive Survey

abstract: The construction of World Models capable of learning, simulating, and reasoning about objective physical laws constitutes a foundational challenge in the pursuit of Artificial General Intelligence. Recent advancements represented by video generation models like Sora have demonstrated the potential of data-driven scaling laws to approximate physical dynamics, while the emerging Unified Multimodal Model (UMM) offers a promising architectural paradigm for integrating perception, language, and reasoning. Despite these advances, the field still lacks a principled theoretical framework that defines the essential properties requisite for a General World Model. In this paper, we propose that a World Model must be grounded in the Trinity of Consistency: Modal Consistency as the semantic interface, Spatial Consistency as the geometric basis, and Temporal Consistency as the causal engine. Through this tripartite lens, we systematically review the evolution of multimodal learning, revealing a trajectory from loosely coupled specialized modules toward unified architectures that enable the synergistic emergence of internal world simulators. To complement this conceptual framework, we introduce CoW-Bench, a benchmark centered on multi-frame reasoning and generation scenarios. CoW-Bench evaluates both video generation models and UMMs under a unified evaluation protocol. Our work establishes a principled pathway toward general world models, clarifying both the limitations of current systems and the architectural requirements for future progress.

keyInsights: Genuine General World Models must possess an integrated understanding of Modal, Spatial, and Temporal consistencies, rather than merely generating visually plausible content.

Current state-of-the-art generative models often exhibit "constraint backoff" and "semantic drift," failing to maintain global world-state, persistent semantics, and coherent inference under dynamic, multi-factor changes.

The most significant performance gaps and differentiations between models emerge in cross-consistency tasks, which demand simultaneous constraint satisfaction across multiple dimensions, highlighting a fundamental "world-model gap."
originalProblem: Existing generative AI models, like large-scale video generators, often exhibit structural hallucinations and temporal inconsistencies, suggesting they mimic pixel statistics rather than genuinely internalizing objective physical principles.

A principled theoretical framework is lacking to define the essential properties required for a true General World Model capable of learning, simulating, and reasoning about the objective physical laws of reality.

Current evaluation benchmarks for generative models often rely on superficial visual metrics, failing to rigorously assess a model's genuine understanding of the world's underlying physics and causality.
results: Closed-source image generation models (e.g., GPT-image-1.5, Nano Banana Pro) achieved the highest overall average scores on CoW-Bench, demonstrating strong internalization of static world regularities.

Open-source video generators generally underperformed across most consistency-sensitive tasks, especially modal grounding and cross-consistency challenges, often relaxing rare constraints or preserving motion while losing identity/attribute consistency.

CoW-Bench revealed that current models excel in local plausibility and single-view settings but sharply degrade in tasks requiring global world-state maintenance, persistent semantics under dynamic changes, and coherent inference across entangled spatio-temporal and modal constraints.
solution: The paper proposes the "Trinity of Consistency"—comprising Modal, Spatial, and Temporal consistency—as a foundational theoretical framework for General World Models.

It systematically reviews the evolution of multimodal learning through this tripartite lens, demonstrating how the deep integration of these consistencies is crucial for the emergence of physical simulation capabilities.

A novel benchmark, CoW-Bench (Consistency of World-models Benchmark), is introduced to rigorously evaluate both video generation models and Unified Multimodal Models against the proposed consistency principles, featuring 18 sub-tasks and 16 atomic failure checks.
paper_summary.summary: This research introduces the "Trinity of Consistency" (Modal, Spatial, Temporal) as a theoretical framework for General World Models and presents CoW-Bench, a diagnostic benchmark. The work reveals that current generative AI models, including state-of-the-art systems, often fail to satisfy fundamental physical and logical consistency constraints, particularly in tasks requiring cross-dimensional reasoning.

title: The Trinity of Consistency as a Defining Principle for General World Models

abstract:
World models have attracted increasing attention in autonomous driving for
their ability to forecast potential future scenarios. In this paper, we propose
BEVWorld, a novel framework that transforms multimodal sensor inputs into a
unified and compact Bird's Eye View (BEV) latent space for holistic environment
modeling. The proposed world model consists of two main components: a
multi-modal tokenizer and a latent BEV sequence diffusion model. The
multi-modal tokenizer first encodes heterogeneous sensory data, and its decoder
reconstructs the latent BEV tokens into LiDAR and surround-view image
observations via ray-casting rendering in a self-supervised manner. This
enables joint modeling and bidirectional encoding-decoding of panoramic imagery
and point cloud data within a shared spatial representation. On top of this,
the latent BEV sequence diffusion model performs temporally consistent
forecasting of future scenes, conditioned on high-level action tokens, enabling
scene-level reasoning over time. Extensive experiments demonstrate the
effectiveness of BEVWorld on autonomous driving benchmarks, showcasing its
capability in realistic future scene generation and its benefits for downstream
tasks such as perception and motion prediction.

keyInsights: Unified multi-modal fusion in a BEV latent space, combining both camera and LiDAR inputs, is crucial for superior environmental representation and reconstruction quality compared to single-modality approaches.
Explicit ray-based rendering, leveraging sensor pose and imaging geometry, is essential for accurately reconstructing multi-view images and LiDAR point clouds from BEV features.

Decoupling the modeling of low-level sensor details from high-level behavioral dynamics within a diffusion framework significantly enhances the realism, temporal consistency, and controllability of future scene generation.
originalProblem: Existing Driving World Models (DWMs) struggled with realistic 3D spatial modeling, often being limited to 2D video generation or single-modality 3D predictions.
Integrating and fusing heterogeneous multi-sensor data (e.g., cameras and LiDAR) into a unified and compact representation for holistic environment understanding remained a significant challenge.
Many DWMs adopted end-to-end architectures that failed to explicitly decouple low-level pixel/voxel details from high-level behavioral dynamics, compromising generation quality and realism.
results: The multi-modal tokenizer, when used for pre-training, improved 3D object detection performance on nuScenes by 8.4% in NDS (from 0.392 to 0.476) and 13.4% in mAP (from 0.253 to 0.387).
The latent BEV sequence diffusion model achieved a Chamfer Distance of 0.73 for 3-second LiDAR predictions on nuScenes, outperforming prior SOTA methods like 4D-Occ (1.40).

BEVWorld generated high-fidelity, action-conditioned multi-view images and LiDAR point clouds, achieving an FID of 19.0 and FVD of 154.0 on nuScenes without manual labeling conditions, demonstrating robust controllability and realism.
solution: BEVWorld introduces a multi-modal tokenizer that unifies diverse camera images and LiDAR point clouds into a compact, low-dimensional Bird’s Eye View (BEV) latent representation, enabling high-fidelity reconstruction via a NeRF-based ray rendering network.
A latent BEV sequence diffusion model forecasts multiple future BEV frames simultaneously, conditioned on ego-vehicle action tokens, to avoid cumulative errors common in auto-regressive approaches.

The framework explicitly decouples low-level sensor data compression and reconstruction (tokenizer) from high-level behavioral dynamics prediction (diffusion model) to improve overall scene generation quality.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Baidu Inc. developed BEVWorld, a multimodal world model for autonomous driving that forecasts future scenes by converting heterogeneous sensor data into a unified Bird’s Eye View (BEV) latent space. The model achieves superior LiDAR prediction quality with a Chamfer Distance of 0.73 for 3-second predictions on nuScenes and significantly boosts downstream 3D object detection performance by 8.4% NDS through its pre-training capabilities.

title: BEVWorld: A Multimodal World Simulator for Autonomous Driving via  Scene-Level BEV Latents

abstract: World models have emerged as a critical frontier in AI research, aiming to enhance large models by infusing them with physical dynamics and world knowledge. The core objective is to enable agents to understand, predict, and interact with complex environments. However, current research landscape remains fragmented, with approaches predominantly focused on injecting world knowledge into isolated tasks, such as visual prediction, 3D estimation, or symbol grounding, rather than establishing a unified definition or framework. While these task-specific integrations yield performance gains, they often lack the systematic coherence required for holistic world understanding. In this paper, we analyze the limitations of such fragmented approaches and propose a unified design specification for world models. We suggest that a robust world model should not be a loose collection of capabilities but a normative framework that integrally incorporates interaction, perception, symbolic reasoning, and spatial representation. This work aims to provide a structured perspective to guide future research toward more general, robust, and principled models of the world.

keyInsights: Injecting world knowledge into isolated tasks results in a lack of systematic coherence, failing to build a foundational, holistic understanding of the world.
Current models often rely on statistical pattern fitting rather than genuine physical comprehension, leading to logical inconsistencies and violations of physical laws in various AI outputs.

Achieving true world understanding necessitates an integrated system that supports active exploration, long-term consistency, and adaptive interaction, not merely aligning outputs with predefined rules.
originalProblem: World model research is fragmented, primarily focusing on injecting knowledge into isolated, task-specific AI systems, lacking systematic coherence.
Existing AI models, including LLMs/VLMs, exhibit a lack of genuine physical understanding, relying on statistical pattern fitting which leads to logical fallacies and physical inconsistencies.
Current task-specific approaches hinder agents from active exploration, discovery, and robust, adaptive responses in complex, unstructured environments.
results: The paper delivers a Unified World Model Framework detailing five core modules: Interaction (unified perception/operation), Reasoning (logical/causal inference), Memory (structured retention), Environment (generative/interactive carrier), and Multimodal Generation (comprehensive feedback).

This framework aims to foster comprehensive world understanding by enabling unified multimodal perception, logical analysis, structured long-term memory, dynamic environment simulation, and closed-loop feedback.

Three critical future research directions are identified: physically-grounded spatiotemporal representation, robust embodied interaction and control, and autonomous reflection with continuous modular evolution.
solution: The paper provides a comprehensive review of existing world model approaches, categorizing and critically analyzing their limitations across reasoning, content generation, and interactive agents.
A "Unified World Model Framework" is proposed, comprising five integrally designed and interconnected modules: Interaction, Reasoning, Memory, Environment, and Multimodal Generation.
This framework emphasizes synergistic, closed-loop operation among its components to enable robust world simulation and active agent intelligence, moving beyond fragmented task-specific integrations.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Peking University, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, HKUST, Tsinghua University, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, and Kuaishou Technology critically assess the fragmented nature of current world model research. They propose a unified framework featuring five interconnected modules—Interaction, Reasoning, Memory, Environment, and Multimodal Generation—to guide future AI advancements toward a more holistic understanding of the physical world and enable robust agent intelligence.

title: Research on World Models Is Not Merely Injecting World Knowledge into Specific Tasks

abstract:
One of the core components of our world models is 'intuitive physics' - an
understanding of objects, space, and causality. This capability enables us to
predict events, plan action and navigate environments, all of which rely on a
composite sense of objecthood. Despite its importance, there is no single,
unified account of objecthood, though multiple theoretical frameworks provide
insights. In the first part of this paper, we present a comprehensive overview
of the main theoretical frameworks in objecthood research - Gestalt psychology,
enactive cognition, and developmental psychology - and identify the core
capabilities each framework attributes to object understanding, as well as what
functional roles they play in shaping world models in biological agents. Given
the foundational role of objecthood in world modelling, understanding
objecthood is also essential in AI. In the second part of the paper, we
evaluate how current AI paradigms approach and test objecthood capabilities
compared to those in cognitive science. We define an AI paradigm as a
combination of how objecthood is conceptualised, the methods used for studying
objecthood, the data utilised, and the evaluation techniques. We find that,
whilst benchmarks can detect that AI systems model isolated aspects of
objecthood, the benchmarks cannot detect when AI systems lack functional
integration across these capabilities, not solving the objecthood challenge
fully. Finally, we explore novel evaluation approaches that align with the
integrated vision of objecthood outlined in this paper. These methods are
promising candidates for advancing from isolated object capabilities toward
general-purpose AI with genuine object understanding in real-world contexts.
title: Cognitive Science-Inspired Evaluation of Core Capabilities for Object  Understanding in AI

abstract: World models aim to understand, remember, and predict dynamic visual environments, yet a unified benchmark for evaluating their fundamental abilities remains lacking. To address this gap, we introduce MIND, the first open-domain closed-loop revisited benchmark for evaluating Memory consIstency and action coNtrol in worlD models. MIND contains 250 high-quality videos at 1080p and 24 FPS, including 100 (first-person) + 100 (third-person) video clips under a shared action space and 25 + 25 clips across varied action spaces covering eight diverse scenes. We design an efficient evaluation framework to measure two core abilities: memory consistency and action control, capturing temporal stability and contextual coherence across viewpoints. Furthermore, we design various action spaces, including different character movement speeds and camera rotation angles, to evaluate the action generalization capability across different action spaces under shared scenes. To facilitate future performance benchmarking on MIND, we introduce MIND-World, a novel interactive Video-to-World baseline. Extensive experiments demonstrate the completeness of MIND and reveal key challenges in current world models, including the difficulty of maintaining long-term memory consistency and generalizing across action spaces. Code: this https URL.

organization_info.name: Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

keyInsights: Models utilizing context memory show improved short-term consistency, but current approaches struggle with maintaining truly long-term memory and reproducing coherent scene content during revisits.

Precise action control remains a challenge for world models, with action accuracy deteriorating even with context memory and in-domain actions, and generalization often failing with altered action spaces.
Third-person perspective modeling presents severe limitations for current world models, which struggle with accurate character-background interaction and maintaining consistent visual relationships.
originalProblem: Existing world model benchmarks primarily prioritize visual quality or physical realism, neglecting critical aspects like long-term memory consistency and precise action control.

Current evaluation methods are often limited to specific domains (e.g., Minecraft) or first-person perspectives, lacking open-domain diversity and comprehensive assessment of character-environment interactions.
There was a lack of unified benchmarks to systematically evaluate how world models generalize across varied action spaces and perspectives.
results: Models employing context memory demonstrated over 4% improvement on the long context memory metric and generated scene consistency compared to those without.
Action accuracy evaluations indicated consistent deterioration in precise action execution, even when context memory was available and actions were within the fine-tuning space.
Action space generalization results showed that models with memory sometimes underperform models without memory when the action space changes, suggesting context disruption.
solution: Introduced MIND, the first open-domain closed-loop revisited benchmark, featuring 250 high-fidelity 1080p/24FPS videos from Unreal Engine 5 across eight diverse scene categories.

Developed a multi-faceted evaluation framework to quantify memory consistency (temporal stability, contextual coherence via revisit strategy) and action control (accuracy and generalization across varied motion scales).
Provided a novel interactive Video-to-World baseline, MIND-World, a high-dynamics, real-time, autoregressive video generation pipeline with a three-stage training process and direct action injection.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed MIND, a new open-domain closed-loop benchmark designed to evaluate the memory consistency and action control capabilities of world models across diverse visual environments and both first-person and third-person perspectives. This benchmark leverages high-fidelity Unreal Engine 5 data to assess how well models retain environmental context and execute precise actions.

title: MIND: Benchmarking Memory Consistency and Action Control in World Models

abstract: The rapid advancement of autonomous systems, including self-driving vehicles and drones, has intensified the need to forge true Spatial Intelligence from multi-modal onboard sensor data. While foundation models excel in single-modal contexts, integrating their capabilities across diverse sensors like cameras and LiDAR to create a unified understanding remains a formidable challenge. This paper presents a comprehensive framework for multi-modal pre-training, identifying the core set of techniques driving progress toward this goal. We dissect the interplay between foundational sensor characteristics and learning strategies, evaluating the role of platform-specific datasets in enabling these advancements. Our central contribution is the formulation of a unified taxonomy for pre-training paradigms: ranging from single-modality baselines to sophisticated unified frameworks that learn holistic representations for advanced tasks like 3D object detection and semantic occupancy prediction. Furthermore, we investigate the integration of textual inputs and occupancy representations to facilitate open-world perception and planning. Finally, we identify critical bottlenecks, such as computational efficiency and model scalability, and propose a roadmap toward general-purpose multi-modal foundation models capable of achieving robust Spatial Intelligence for real-world deployment.

organization_info.name: Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics

keyInsights: Representation learning in autonomous systems is evolving from single-modality self-supervision to cross-modal synergy and unified pre-training frameworks, leveraging foundation models.

Modern datasets increasingly incorporate planning trajectories, logic, and language, facilitating the training of embodied AI and Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, alongside a growing reliance on synthetic and generative data.

Achieving 'true Spatial Intelligence' requires shifting from specialized, task-specific models to holistic, multi-modal foundation models capable of learning generalizable world knowledge and enabling end-to-end planning.
originalProblem: Autonomous systems require deep environmental understanding, but face challenges integrating heterogeneous sensor data (camera, LiDAR, radar, event cameras) for holistic perception.
Large-scale datasets for autonomous systems rely heavily on costly manual annotations, leading to scalability and generalization bottlenecks.
While foundation models excel in single-modal contexts, their effective integration across diverse, heterogeneous sensors for autonomous systems remains a significant challenge.
results: Quantitative analysis on benchmarks like nuScenes demonstrates that multi-modal and unified pre-training significantly outperform single-modality baselines in 3D object detection and LiDAR semantic segmentation.
Foundation models, particularly Vision-Language Models (VLMs), enable auto-labeling and self-supervised 3D occupancy prediction, addressing annotation bottlenecks.

Generative world models, such as OccWorld and GenAD, predict 4D occupancy flows conditioned on ego-actions, showing improved planning fidelity and safety, moving towards unified Vision-Action (VA) and Vision-Language-Action (VLA) frameworks.
solution: Developed a unified taxonomy categorizing multi-modal pre-training techniques, from single-modality baselines to unified frameworks and foundation model integration.
Performed an extensive literature review and chronological analysis of state-of-the-art methods, synthesizing empirical performance on key 3D perception tasks.

Identified critical bottlenecks and proposed a clear roadmap for future research, including physically consistent world simulators and 4D semantic-geometric unification, aiming for general-purpose multi-modal foundation models.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Zhejiang University, National University of Singapore, and collaborators present a detailed roadmap for multi-modal data pre-training in autonomous systems, categorizing methods and identifying challenges to achieve robust Spatial Intelligence. The work synthesizes an evolutionary trajectory from single-modality to unified foundation models, underscoring their empirical advantages in 3D perception and generative world modeling.

title: Forging Spatial Intelligence: A Roadmap of Multi-Modal Data Pre-Training for Autonomous Systems

abstract: World models (WMs) are intended to serve as internal simulators of the real world that enable agents to understand, anticipate, and act upon complex environments. Existing WM benchmarks remain narrowly focused on next-state prediction and visual fidelity, overlooking the richer simulation capabilities required for intelligent behavior. To address this gap, we introduce WR-Arena, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating WMs along three fundamental dimensions of next world simulation: (i) Action Simulation Fidelity, the ability to interpret and follow semantically meaningful, multi-step instructions and generate diverse counterfactual rollouts; (ii) Long-horizon Forecast, the ability to sustain accurate, coherent, and physically plausible simulations across extended interactions; and (iii) Simulative Reasoning and Planning, the ability to support goal-directed reasoning by simulating, comparing, and selecting among alternative futures in both structured and open-ended environments. We build a task taxonomy and curate diverse datasets designed to probe these capabilities, moving beyond single-turn and perceptual evaluations. Through extensive experiments with state-of-the-art WMs, our results expose a substantial gap between current models and human-level hypothetical reasoning, and establish WR-Arena as both a diagnostic tool and a guideline for advancing next-generation world models capable of robust understanding, forecasting, and purposeful action. The code is available at this https URL.
authors: PAN Team Institute of Foundation Models

full_authors.full_name: PAN Team Institute of Foundation Models

keyInsights: Current world models face significant challenges in sustaining coherent simulations over long horizons, with none exceeding 65% on consistency metrics due to error accumulation.

Performing environment-level interventions is substantially harder for WMs than agent-centric manipulations, with models averaging 11.5% lower accuracy and none surpassing 60% on environment simulation tasks.

Effective planning requires WMs to produce 'semantically actionable rollouts' rather than just perceptually plausible ones, indicating the need for explicit alignment between action representations and state transitions.
originalProblem: Existing world model (WM) benchmarks primarily focus on low-level metrics like next-state prediction or pixel-level fidelity, often in single-turn or short-term settings.
Current evaluation frameworks inadequately assess WMs' ability to maintain long-horizon dynamics, interpret multi-step instructions, or support goal-directed simulative reasoning for planning.
There is a gap in determining if WMs can function as robust internal 'simulation sandboxes' for complex decision-making and purposeful action, rather than just passive predictors.
results: PAN, a world model incorporating action-state aligned fine-tuning, achieved the best performance on Long-horizon Forecast metrics, demonstrating a flatter degradation curve and mitigating error accumulation more effectively.

MiniMax showed strong overall performance in Action Simulation Fidelity (72.33% on Agent Simulation, 51.67% on Environment Simulation), while PAN improved significantly over general video generators due to targeted training.

Integrated with a VLM planner, PAN yielded substantial gains in simulative reasoning, improving trajectory-level success by 26.33% in open-ended settings and 23.40% in structured environments compared to a VLM-only baseline.
solution: The World Reasoning Arena (WR-Arena) benchmark was introduced to evaluate WMs across three dimensions: Action Simulation Fidelity, Long-horizon Forecast, and Simulative Reasoning and Planning.
A structured taxonomy of evaluation tasks and curated datasets were developed, leveraging LLMs to propose actions and VLMs as judges for multi-step scenarios.

The methodology involves assessing a WM's capacity for interpreting high-level instructions, maintaining coherent simulations over extended interactions, and supporting iterative planning through VLM-WM collaboration.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the PAN Team at MBZUAI introduced WR-Arena, a new benchmark designed to assess advanced capabilities of world models beyond short-term prediction, including action simulation fidelity, long-horizon forecast, and simulative reasoning for planning. Evaluations revealed that current models struggle with error accumulation in long-horizon simulations and consistently performing environment-level interventions, with action-state aligned models showing improved planning performance.

title: World Reasoning Arena

abstract: The rapid progress in embodied artificial intelligence has highlighted the necessity for more advanced and integrated models that can perceive, interpret, and predict environmental dynamics. In this context, World Models (WMs) have been introduced to provide embodied agents with the abilities to anticipate future environmental states and fill in knowledge gaps, thereby enhancing agents' ability to plan and execute actions. However, when dealing with embodied agents it is fundamental to ensure that predictions are safe for both the agent and the environment. In this article, we conduct a comprehensive literature review of World Models in the domains of autonomous driving and robotics, with a specific focus on the safety implications of scene and control generation tasks. Our review is complemented by an empirical analysis, wherein we collect and examine predictions from state-of-the-art models, identify and categorize common faults (herein referred to as pathologies), and provide a quantitative evaluation of the results.

organization_info.name: University of Modena and Reggio Emilia
